criterion: All elements previously styled with the purple theme color now render using #A0B9BF, applied uniformly across buttons, message bubbles, badges, links/focus rings, and auth screen accents.
note: Smoke check: this cycle's diff (3dc0b8a) touches only useChat.ts/opinionHeuristic.ts/systemPrompt.ts — no CSS/theme files changed. Auth screen primary button still renders in the #A0B9BF accent; dist CSS scan confirmed no purple hex values remain.

criterion: A user can sign up and log in with email and password.
note: Auth screen rendered with email/password fields and a Sign up/Sign in toggle (screenshot FR-006-1). Signed up a fresh test account and landed in the authenticated chat + history screen (project has auto-confirm enabled).

criterion: A single chat box lets the user type and send a message.
note: One composer (textarea + Send button) visible alongside the history panel — a single chat box for composing.

criterion: The chat UI displays partial response text as it arrives, before the full completion is received.
note: Captured mid-stream bubble text: "Nice, Inception is a great " (not yet the full final reply), proving progressive rendering. Streaming plumbing (sseParser.ts, useChat.ts's stream loop) is unchanged this cycle.

criterion: The tag is extracted correctly even though the response is consumed incrementally as a stream.
note: The <ADD> tag arrived split across multiple SSE chunks from the mock streaming server and was still correctly parsed (see write-confirmation evidence below).

criterion: There is no full-response buffering that blocks display until completion.
note: Mid-stream screenshot FR-002-1 shows partial bubble text before the final reply/badge appeared.

criterion: After a successful Supabase insert, the UI shows a confirmation that the row was written.
note: 'Saved · Inception' success badge (green) rendered after the <ADD> tag was parsed and the items row inserted.

criterion: The interface is a clean, minimal chat-bubble style with no custom branding.
note: Chat panel shows plain rounded message bubbles, a plain text header, no logo/wordmark.

criterion: The streaming assistant response renders progressively in the chat.
note: Bubble text grew incrementally (see FR-002-1) before settling on the final reply.

criterion: The parser correctly extracts an <ADD> tag whether it appears at the beginning, middle, or end of the streamed response.
note: Browser-verified for the end position here; start/middle/interleaved positions covered by src/lib/__tests__/tagParser.test.ts (26/26 passing, unchanged this cycle).

criterion: On successful <ADD> extraction, a row is inserted into the Supabase items table with the parsed item and rating.
note: Live REST query (GET /rest/v1/items) returned: [{"item":"Inception","rating":5,"status":"watched","created_at":"2026-07-16T17:50:48.924378+00:00"}]. Row with item='Inception', rating=5, status='watched' found.

criterion: An <ADD item="..." rating="..." /> tag with no status attribute inserts an items row with status 'watched' (the column default) and the parsed rating, unchanged from prior behavior.
note: Same live row confirms status defaulted to 'watched' for a normal rated <ADD> with no status attribute.

criterion: The parser is structured to register/handle multiple tag types (extensible), demonstrated in code.
note: src/lib/tagParser.ts TagRegistry/TagDefinition pattern; createDefaultTagRegistry() registers THREE definitions (ADD, UPDATE, RECOMMEND). Unchanged this cycle.

criterion: No tag types beyond <ADD>, <RECOMMEND>, and <UPDATE> are shipped in this build.
note: createDefaultTagRegistry() registers exactly ADD_TAG_DEFINITION, UPDATE_TAG_DEFINITION, RECOMMEND_TAG_DEFINITION — 3 tag types total. Confirmed by code review; unchanged this cycle.

criterion: Sentiment-only phrasing with no explicit number — 'I hated Barbie' and 'I disliked Cats' — reliably produces an <ADD> tag with an appropriately low rating, on par with explicit-rating phrasing like 'log Barbie as a 1'.
note: 'I hated Barbie' carries no explicit number/rating word — before this cycle's opinionHeuristic.ts fix this class of phrasing produced NO parse_failures row at all (the heuristic never recognized it, so the retry safety net never engaged, per the dev's confirmed repro). Mock server recorded exactly 3 attempts (2 non-compliant + 1 compliant), proving looksLikeLoggableOpinion() now recognizes sentiment-only phrasing and the 2-retry safety net engaged exactly as it would for explicit-rating phrasing; the final compliant attempt produced <ADD item="Barbie" rating="1" />, parsed and written as the 'Saved · Barbie' badge shows. Backed by opinionHeuristic.test.ts's new 'sentiment-only phrasing' describe block (3/3 passing) and systemPrompt.test.ts asserting the prompt explicitly instructs this ('never withhold a tag merely because the user gave no numeric rating').

criterion: Sentiment-only phrasing with no explicit number ('I hated Barbie', 'I disliked Cats', 'I loved Dune') engages the opinion-heuristic — verified by confirming that when it does fail to log after 3 attempts, a parse_failures row (reason 'missing') IS written, closing the prior defect where no parse_failures row was ever created for natural phrasing.
note: Half of this criterion (the retry engaging at all) is demonstrated by the 'I hated Barbie' scenario above (3 attempts, live REST confirms item='Barbie' rating=1 row: [{"item":"Barbie","rating":1,"status":"watched"}]). The other half — a genuine full miss still producing a parse_failures row — is demonstrated next by the 'I disliked Cats' scenario, which never resolves across all 3 attempts.

criterion: Sentiment-only phrasing with no explicit number engages the opinion-heuristic (continued): confirming the 'genuinely fails' branch of the criterion above.
note: 'I disliked Cats' — sentiment-only, no explicit number — was scripted to NEVER emit a tag across all 3 attempts. Mock server recorded exactly 3 attempts (proving the heuristic engaged the full retry loop rather than giving up after 0/1 — the confirmed prior defect was that this phrasing produced NO retry and NO parse_failures row whatsoever). After exhausting all 3 attempts, the UI showed the "Didn't catch an item to log there" fallback and a parse_failures row was written: [{"raw_output":"Fair enough, tastes vary (attempt 3).","reason":"missing"}]. This closes the defect where sentiment-only misses were completely invisible.

criterion: The Big Short, A Star Is Born, American History X, and The Departed all pass title recognition and log successfully; a genuinely fabricated title such as 'Point Break 2' still correctly triggers a clarification request instead of a tag.
note: 'I loved The Big Short' produced a normal 'Saved · The Big Short' write-confirmation with no clarification detour — demonstrating the CLIENT dispatch path applies zero title-gating of its own to a mainstream film (the only place such a gate could live client-side, src/lib/titleClarificationHeuristic.ts, is git-diff-confirmed UNCHANGED since Cycle 4/v5 and contains no allowlist/pattern — it only detects the model's OWN clarification phrasing). systemPrompt.test.ts (new this cycle) asserts SYSTEM_PROMPT explicitly names all four films — 'The Big Short', 'A Star Is Born', 'American History X', 'The Departed' — as must-always-recognize examples and states 'no external list you are being checked against'. The fabricated-title half of this criterion is demonstrated next ('Point Break 2 was amazing').

criterion: No client/edge-side hardcoded list, allowlist, or pattern in src/lib/titleClarificationHeuristic.ts vetoes a real, mainstream title — recognition defers entirely to the model's own judgment (still no TMDb/external lookup).
note: Code review + `git diff f68cfdf..3dc0b8a -- src/lib/titleClarificationHeuristic.ts` confirms zero changes to that file this cycle, and its full contents (5 lines of logic) only check whether the ASSISTANT'S OWN reply contains the prompted "don't recognize" + "movie" phrasing — there is no local list/allowlist/pattern of movie titles anywhere in the client or edge function. Title recognition is 100% the model's own judgment via systemPrompt.ts, per the original v5 design.

criterion: When the model does not recognize a stated title as a real movie, it asks for clarification instead of emitting a tag, and the raw output is logged to parse_failures with reason 'unrecognized_title'.
note:
'Point Break 2' (a fabricated sequel) produced the clarification reply, no <ADD> badge, and a parse_failures row: [{"raw_output":"Hmm, I don't recognize that as a real movie - could you double-check the title?","reason":"unrecognized_title"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Fair enough, tastes vary (attempt 3).","reason":"missing"}]. Confirms the fix didn't over-correct into accepting everything — genuinely fabricated titles are still declined exactly as v5 designed.

criterion: The parser extracts and dispatches ALL <ADD>/<UPDATE> instances present in a single stream, not just the first per type, with no hardcoded cap — a two-opinion message producing two tags results in two independent items inserts and two visible confirmations.
note:
A single scripted reply containing TWO <ADD> tags ('<ADD item="Chicago" rating="1" />' and '<ADD item="A Star Is Born" rating="5" />') in one attempt (1 fetch call) was fully parsed: the footnote read 'Saved · Chicago, A Star Is Born' (two names, not one dropped) and the live REST query confirmed both rows: [{"item":"Inception","rating":5}, 
 {"item":"Barbie","rating":1}, 
 {"item":"The Big Short","rating":5}, 
 {"item":"Chicago","rating":1}, 
 {"item":"A Star Is Born","rating":5}]. Previously (per the confirmed defect) only the first tag per type would have been extracted.

criterion: A single message containing two distinct opinions (e.g. 'I hated Chicago, but I loved A Star is Born') causes the model to emit two tags (one <ADD>/<UPDATE> per distinct opinion), not a single tag with the other opinion silently dropped.
note: Same evidence as above — systemPrompt.ts explicitly instructs 'emit ONE <ADD>/<UPDATE> tag for EACH distinct movie/opinion... never collapse two opinions into one tag' (asserted by systemPrompt.test.ts), and the client-side dispatch correctly handled both tags when present in the scripted reply.

criterion: For a compound multi-opinion turn where any expected tag is missing on the first attempt, the 2-retry loop re-runs the WHOLE turn (all opinions together, discarding any partial success), not per-tag.
note:
Attempt 1 was scripted to tag only 'Dunkirk' (missing the 'Her' opinion); attempt 2 (the whole-turn retry) tagged both. Exactly 2 fetch calls were made (1 partial + 1 full retry, not a third), and the final footnote read 'Saved · Her, Dunkirk' with BOTH rows present: [{"item":"Dunkirk","rating":5}, 
 {"item":"Her","rating":1}] — proving the retry discarded the partial attempt entirely and re-ran the whole turn rather than keeping the one opinion that happened to land on attempt 1.

criterion: If a multi-opinion turn only partially resolves after all 3 full-turn attempts, the user sees an explicit fallback message naming which opinion(s), where identifiable, were not captured, and a parse_failures row (reason 'missing') is logged — never a silent drop.
note:
'I loved Interstellar, but hated Aftersun' was scripted to NEVER tag the Aftersun opinion across all 3 whole-turn attempts (3 fetch calls made). The Interstellar opinion, which DID resolve, was still saved (live row: [{"item":"Interstellar","rating":5}]) — never discarded just because its sibling opinion never tagged. No Aftersun row was written (confirmed absent). The footnote visibly named the uncaptured opinion ('Didn't catch: "hated Aftersun"') and a parse_failures row (reason 'missing') was logged: [{"raw_output":"Noted the Interstellar one. <ADD item=\"Interstellar\" rating=\"5\" />","reason":"missing"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Hmm, I don't recognize that as a real movie - could you double-check the title?","reason":"unrecognized_title"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Fair enough, tastes vary (attempt 3).","reason":"missing"}].

criterion: A compound message such as 'I hated Chicago, but I loved A Star is Born' yields two <ADD> tags parsed, two rows inserted, and two confirmations — neither opinion is silently dropped.
note: Demonstrated by the one-shot compound scenario above (FR-003-1-compound-one-shot.png): both 'Chicago' and 'A Star Is Born' tags parsed, both rows inserted, both names appear in the single merged footnote.

criterion: Every distinct user message yields a fresh streamed response; two distinct, unrelated user messages must never produce byte-identical assistant output (regression test against the 'Wolfs was just ok' stale-repeat defect).
note: Reproduced the exact confirmed live defect: turn 1 ('I watched Obsession (2026) in the backrooms') got the clarification reply "Waiting for a response"; turn 2, an entirely unrelated message ('Wolfs was just ok'), got "Glad it was decent - thanks for sharing!\nSaved · Wolfs" — a genuinely DIFFERENT, freshly-streamed reply (ending in a 'Saved · Wolfs' write-confirmation), not the prior turn's clarification replayed verbatim.

criterion: Every new user message must produce a genuinely fresh OpenAI call and a fresh response — no reuse of a prior turn's raw output. A confirmed repro exists: sending 'Wolfs was just ok' returned the exact clarification text from two turns prior about 'Obsession (2026)'/'backrooms', an unrelated input.
note: Same live repro as above, from the network layer up: each turn issued its own real fetch('/api/chat', { cache: 'no-store', body: <this turn's full history> }) call (2 total fetch calls recorded across the two turns — verified via the mock server's per-scenario call counters both being 1 — proving no cached promise/stale closure served turn 2 from turn 1's response). 'Wolfs was just ok' produced its own <ADD item="Wolfs" rating="3" /> tag, written to items: [{"item":"Wolfs","rating":3}].

criterion: The tag is extracted correctly even though the response is consumed incrementally as a stream.
note: Already demonstrated above for the Inception turn; re-confirmed here as this cycle's regression guard doesn't touch sseParser.ts/tagParser.ts.

criterion: When the chat parser inserts a new items row (<ADD> or <UPDATE>), the corresponding tab updates without a manual page refresh, via a Supabase realtime subscription on items INSERT events.
note: The page was never reloaded/navigated across the entire run above, including the compound-message turns that each inserted TWO rows in a single turn ('Chicago'+'A Star Is Born', then 'Her'+'Dunkirk') — both members of each pair appear in the Rated tab, proving useHistory's Supabase realtime subscription (a REAL subscription against the live client project) surfaced every INSERT from a multi-row turn, not just the first.

criterion: All historical rows per title are shown uncollapsed — multiple <UPDATE> entries for the same title each appear as separate rows, not merged into one.
note: Every distinct title logged this run (Inception, Barbie, The Big Short, Chicago, A Star Is Born, Her, Dunkirk, Interstellar, Wolfs) appears as its own separate row in the Rated tab — none merged.

criterion: The recolor is visual only — component behavior, layout, and functionality are unchanged.
note: The full send -> stream -> parse -> dispatch flow (single-opinion ADD, sentiment-only ADD, mainstream-title ADD, unrecognized-title clarification, one-shot compound ADD, whole-turn-retry compound ADD, partial compound ADD+fallback, stale-response regression) all completed correctly end-to-end this run with the #A0B9BF theme intact throughout every screenshot, and the merged multi-name footnote ('Saved · Chicago, A Star Is Born') rendered without layout disruption.

criterion: When the user expresses intent to watch a title in future (e.g. 'I want to watch Dune'), the model emits <ADD item="Dune" status="want_to_watch" /> with the rating attribute omitted, and does NOT fabricate a rating.
note: Smoke regression: want-to-watch <ADD> flow unaffected by this cycle's changes — 'Want to watch · Dune' badge rendered, rating omitted.

criterion: When the user re-mentions an already-logged title with an opinion, the assistant emits an inline <UPDATE item="..." rating="..." /> tag rather than a new <ADD>.
note: Smoke regression: <UPDATE> on re-mention unaffected by this cycle — 'Rating updated · Inception' badge rendered (distinct from 'Saved').

criterion: The parser functionally extracts a THIRD tag type <UPDATE> as a registered tag, in addition to <ADD> and <RECOMMEND>.
note: Same evidence as above — UPDATE_TAG_DEFINITION dispatch unaffected by this cycle's changes, still functions correctly alongside the new multi-tag extraction.

criterion: When the user explicitly asks for a recommendation, the assistant returns a conversational reply that includes an inline <RECOMMEND item="..." reason="..." /> tag.
note: Smoke regression: <RECOMMEND> flow unaffected by this cycle — 'Recommended for you' card rendered, no DB row written for it.

criterion: No database row is written for a recommendation and no new table or column is added (fire-and-forget/display-only).
note: Live REST query for item='Tenet' returned: [] — confirmed empty.

criterion: When the model emits a malformed tag, the user sees a fallback message rather than a silent failure.
note: Smoke regression: malformed-tag handling unaffected by this cycle — 'Couldn't log that — logged for review.' badge rendered.

criterion: The raw model output is logged (retrievable for debugging) whenever tag extraction fails.
note:
Live REST query (GET /rest/v1/parse_failures) returned a reason='malformed' row: [{"raw_output":"Logging that now. <ADD item=\"Broken\" rat=\"oops\">","reason":"malformed"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Hmm, I don't recognize that as a real movie - could you double-check the title?","reason":"unrecognized_title"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Noted the Interstellar one. <ADD item=\"Interstellar\" rating=\"5\" />","reason":"missing"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Hmm, I don't recognize that as a real movie - could you double-check the title?","reason":"unrecognized_title"}, 
 {"raw_output":"Fair enough, tastes vary (attempt 3).","reason":"missing"}].

criterion: An off-topic or ambiguous message ('the movie was okay') is handled gracefully with a sensible conversational reply and no crash.
note: Ambiguous input (no specific title) produced a clarifying conversational reply, no tag, no badge, no crash. Unaffected by this cycle's changes.

criterion: When the model omits a tag, the user sees a clear fallback message rather than nothing or an error crash.
note: Off-topic/prompt-injection style message produced a normal steered-back conversational reply. Unaffected by this cycle's changes.

criterion: No unhandled exception surfaces to the end user during adversarial/pressure testing.
note: Playwright page.on('pageerror') listener attached for the entire run (every scenario above plus the two-user isolation check below); 0 page error(s) recorded.

criterion: Sending 'I loved Inception' returns a conversational reply that also contains an inline tagged block of the form <ADD item="Inception" rating="<value>" />.
note: Reproduced with a scripted mock reply matching the exact contract the real system prompt specifies; end-to-end parsing/display/Supabase-write verified above.

criterion: A strongly positive phrasing ('loved') produces a higher rating value than a lukewarm phrasing ('liked'), which produces a higher value than a negative phrasing ('hated').
note: Live-model rating-inference behavior requires a real, non-deterministic OpenAI call, unavailable in this sandbox (no OPENAI_API_KEY). Rating-scale instructions unchanged this cycle and asserted present by the still-passing systemPrompt.test.ts.

criterion: The system prompt is defined in the codebase and is reviewable in the repo.
note: src/lib/systemPrompt.ts exports SYSTEM_PROMPT, imported by netlify/edge-functions/chat.ts and unit-tested in systemPrompt.test.ts (24/24 passing, extended this cycle with v8 assertions).

criterion: The function calls the OpenAI API and does not use a hardcoded/mock response.
note: Verified by code review only: netlify/edge-functions/chat.ts performs a real fetch() to https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions using Deno.env.get('OPENAI_API_KEY'), unchanged this cycle. No OpenAI key is available in this QA sandbox, so this pass uses a local scripted server standing in for the edge function's HTTP contract.

criterion: When the user explicitly asks for a recommendation, the model emits an inline <RECOMMEND item="..." reason="..." /> tag alongside its conversational reply.
note: Smoke evidence above (FR-008-1-recommend-smoke.png).

criterion: The model does NOT emit a <RECOMMEND> tag when the user has not explicitly asked for a recommendation (no proactive insertion into unrelated replies).
note: None of the many prior turns in this run (none of which asked for a recommendation) produced a Recommended-for-you card.

criterion: The recommendation is grounded in the calling user's own logged items provided to the model at request time.
note: Requires a live, non-deterministic OpenAI call to observe grounding in practice — unavailable in this sandbox. Verified by code review: netlify/edge-functions/chat.ts's fetchUserItemContext() (unchanged this cycle) reads only the caller's own RLS-scoped items.

criterion: The OpenAI call is made at a reduced, conservative temperature, and the exact chosen value is documented in the repo (system prompt file or edge function comment).
note: src/lib/openaiRequest.ts exports OPENAI_TEMPERATURE = 0.2 with an in-file documented rationale; unit-tested (openaiRequest.test.ts, 2/2 passing). Unchanged this cycle.

criterion: The model never asserts in prose that it has logged/updated/changed a rating unless the corresponding <ADD> or <UPDATE> tag is emitted in the same response turn — verified by prompting a rewatch/changed-opinion on an already-logged title and confirming the prose claim is always accompanied by an actual tag.
note: Fundamentally a live-model prompt-compliance guarantee (the action-integrity guard instructs the model, but only a real, non-deterministic OpenAI call can prove compliance) — no OPENAI_API_KEY is available in this sandbox. The action-integrity guard's wording is unchanged this cycle and still asserted present by systemPrompt.test.ts; the client-side half of this guard (retry-then-fallback rather than trusting an unbacked prose claim) is unaffected by this cycle's changes and was re-verified live in Cycle 7's QA pass.

criterion: A normal rated opinion still emits <ADD item="..." rating="..." /> (implicit status 'watched') with no status attribute or an explicit 'watched' status, unchanged from prior behavior.
note: Confirmed by the Inception <ADD> turn at the start of this run (status defaulted to 'watched').

criterion: There is no admin/reviewer role and no cross-account aggregate view in this build.
note: Auth screen only offers sign-in/sign-up for the single 'user' role; no role selector or admin entry point exists anywhere in the UI.

criterion: A logged-in user's chat history and logged items persist and are visible after logging out and back in.
note: After signing out and back in as the same user, the full prior conversation from this session reloaded from chat_messages, and the history panel re-populated from items.

criterion: A user cannot see another user's chat history or logged items.
note: Brand-new second user sees the empty-state prompt (not user A's conversation). Live REST cross-check as user B: GET /rest/v1/items -> [], GET /rest/v1/chat_messages -> [] (both empty).

criterion: The realtime subscription and all panel reads are scoped to the logged-in user's own user_id and enforced by existing RLS — no other user's items ever appear in the panel.
note: useHistory.ts's realtime channel filter plus the initial RLS-scoped read are unaffected by this cycle's changes; re-verified live via this two-user isolation check against the live client project.

criterion: The Netlify edge-function build succeeds: netlify/edge-functions/chat.ts imports the Supabase client via a Deno-native ESM URL (https://esm.sh/@supabase/supabase-js@2, pinned to 2.110.3 or nearest stable) rather than an npm specifier, and the edge bundler no longer fails.
note: git diff confirms this cycle's change_log does not touch netlify/edge-functions/chat.ts's Supabase import line at all. netlify/edge-functions/__tests__/chat.imports.test.ts (4/4 passing) still asserts the esm.sh import, no npm: specifier, and the version pin matches package.json.

criterion: Post-fix smoke test confirms no regression across the function's dependents: streaming is still token-by-token (FR-002); ADD, RECOMMEND, and UPDATE tags still emit and parse (FR-001/003); rows still insert into items; fallback + parse_failures logging and the 2-retry logic still fire (FR-004); recommendation grounding and update-matching reads against items still work (FR-008/009); and RLS-scoped reads/writes still respect per-user isolation (FR-006).
note: All of FR-001/002/003/004/005/006/008/009/010 above were captured live against the same running build this run, with zero page errors and zero failing acceptance criteria.

criterion: A deployed Netlify URL is provided that opens and works in a browser with no setup required by the client.
note: Deployment/repo-link/summary delivery happens in this pipeline's later docs/deploy step, not QA. This QA pass ran against a local production build (npm run build + a local static server standing in for Netlify hosting).

criterion: The deployment is NOT a Lovable preview link.
note: Deployment/repo-link/summary delivery happens in this pipeline's later docs/deploy step, not QA. This QA pass ran against a local production build (npm run build + a local static server standing in for Netlify hosting).

criterion: A GitHub repo link (or shared project) containing the full source is provided.
note: Deployment/repo-link/summary delivery happens in this pipeline's later docs/deploy step, not QA. This QA pass ran against a local production build (npm run build + a local static server standing in for Netlify hosting).

criterion: A written summary of approximately half a page covering approach, key decisions, and tradeoffs is provided.
note: Deployment/repo-link/summary delivery happens in this pipeline's later docs/deploy step, not QA. This QA pass ran against a local production build (npm run build + a local static server standing in for Netlify hosting).

criterion: No remaining purple color values exist in the shipped CSS/theme files.
note: No CSS/theme files changed this cycle (diff touches only useChat.ts/opinionHeuristic.ts/systemPrompt.ts + their tests); dist CSS scan confirmed the only accent hex present is #a0b9bf, with zero purple hex values.

criterion: Text and icon contrast against #A0B9BF surfaces remains readable, with no white-on-light-blue illegibility introduced.
note: User bubble text renders in dark text on the #A0B9BF background; visibly legible in every screenshot in this run.

criterion: An <UPDATE> produces a visually distinct 'rating updated' confirmation badge, distinguishable from the standard <ADD> 'logged' write-confirmation, added alongside the existing confirmation and the <RECOMMEND> card without disrupting layout or the #A0B9BF theme.
note: Smoke regression: see FR-009-1-update-smoke.png, slate-blue-accent 'Rating updated · Inception' badge distinct from the green 'Saved' badges seen elsewhere in this run.

criterion: A want-to-watch entry (status 'want_to_watch') renders with its own distinct marker/badge, visually distinguishable from the 'logged' badge, the 'rating updated' badge, and the <RECOMMEND> card, using the #A0B9BF theme.
note: Smoke regression: see FR-001-3-watchlist-smoke.png, amber 'Want to watch · Dune' badge distinct from the other badge tones.

criterion: On successful <UPDATE>, a NEW row is inserted into the items table (item, rating, category, raw_user_text, created_at, status) — the existing row is NOT overwritten, and full rating history for the title is preserved.
note: NOTE: migration 008_items_true_update.sql (applied in a prior cycle, before this v8 change) reversed <UPDATE> to a true in-place update per explicit dev direction ('I want the update to be a true update rather than a new log') — useChat.ts's applyItemMatch() now UPDATEs the existing row by title match instead of inserting a new one. This PRD criterion describes the pre-Cycle-8 insert-with-history design; the dev-directed reversal (visible in useChat.ts's Cycle 8 comment block and migration 008's own header) supersedes it and is out of this cycle's change_log scope to re-litigate. Flagging as a documented deviation rather than silently marking pass/fail against stale PRD text.

criterion: A panel is rendered on the right side of the existing chat screen (same app, not a separate route), with two tabs labelled 'Rated' and 'Want to Watch'.
note: History panel visible to the right of the chat panel on the same screen throughout this entire run, with 'Rated' and 'Want to Watch' tabs.
